DATE/TIME: Day 12
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Day 12
Apr 15, 2019, 1:00:15 PM
Day 12
Multi-modal, wet weather riding. Loaded up to ride to the train station and abandoned that plan on the way down the driveway. Rerouted to the bus station because who wants to arrive at the train soaking wet? I guess I do, because after I checked my luggage I took advantage of a break in the weather to go grab breakfast. And the sky opened up on me, so a soggy train ride after all. I had dried out by the time the train had reached Charlotte, but the weather had only gotten worse. Rode or swam, depending on your point of view, from the train station to the bus depot and rode the bus to the outskirts of Charlotte. It had fortunately stopped raining long enough for me to bike from the bus stop to Mom's where, after a quick change into dry clothes, @skyflyer whisked me away via car to my brother's birthday dinner. Then to theater, and then, late at night, back at Mom's, she hadn't had time do her #30daysofbiking riding, so we took a short ride through the neighborhood before the clock struck midnight. A dry ride, for once that day, so that was a nice finish to Day 12: I Made It Through The Rain

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATE/TIME: Day 13
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Day 13
Apr 15, 2019, 5:30:27 PM
Day 13
Woke up to more rain, storms. They weren't supposed to last all day, but it was iffy as to whether we'd get a break in the weather before heading off to see @sarahj kick theatrical butt as Penny on the final night of #hairspray. We got a suitable gap in the rain, so Mom and I headed out, and were tracked down by @skyflyer, who needed a chili-fix, asap, so we all headed back to the house for chili and a frenzied game of Pit before heading off for a second night at the theater. Got about 3 miles of riding in and another soundtrack on my to-buy list on #30daysofbiking Day 13: Give My Regards to Broadway

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATE/TIME: Day 14
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Day 14
Apr 15, 2019, 5:31:14 PM
Day 14
A few years back my brother came to Raleigh to do a #S24O camping ride. He said Raleigh was way hillier than Charlotte, and I said that was impossible as Raleigh was not terribly hilly and Charlotte was closer to the mountains. I remembered that conversation while biking back to the train station and marveling at how my fully loaded bike was cruising easily down the flat, flat, Charlotte roads. I think it was 5 miles in before I hit an incline noticeable enough to downshift. It's not often that I can roll along at speed and still have enough air left to sing showtunes. You're welcome, Charlotte. No charge for the concert. To the train station in plenty of time, well before today's storms hit, and back to Raleigh, where I had gained even more ground on the approaching storms. I had gained so much of a lead over the rain that I was able to pop into @slims_tagrams for some music by @dana_sullivan and #maldora and still make it home before the rain hit. Add to this weekend's expenses the fact that I will soon be needing to pick up the soundtrack to #hairspray and @dana_sullivan's new album. Also Raleigh is kind of hilly. Who knew? #30daysofbiking Day 14: A Little Traveling Music, Please

Day 15
Every taxing climb on the way into work is a pleasant coast on the way home, so midway through a 2-way commute is a good place to be. Doesn't work quite like that for #30daysofbiking. I won't be coasting to the finish, but I feel like my chances of getting there are pretty good on Day 15: Halfway Over the Hill

See it in all of it's vertical glory (oops) here:

Day 17

Day 17
Turns out on a 7.6 mile commute I can listen to the original Broadway cast of #Hairspray sing You Can't Stop the Beat 8 times, if I hit the "back" button before they get to the bonus, "hidden," drunk driving PSA track. Curiously, if I don't listen to You Can't Stop the Beat on repeat, my commute takes long enough for me to have listened to You Can't Stop the Beat 9 times. It's a #paradox.#30daysofbiking #morecowbell Day 17: Turn the Radio Up.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Day 18
Apr 19, 2019, 11:25:18 AM
Day 18
We've come a long way since day 2, when I was too much of a wiener and never mustered up the energy to bike a chilly, morning commute, and had to ketchup on my miles in the afternoon. Now the temperature is edging into the 80's, and I've already exhausted all my puns. Something about buns, maybe? Never mind, today is all about sweaty bike rides, sizzling music, and hot dogs on #30daysofbiking Day 18: You're Looking Hot Tonight

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATE/TIME: Day 19
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Day 19
Apr 19, 2019, 8:32:47 PM
Day 19
It wasn't until an e-mail about where in the office to take shelter from a storm that I realized why people were concerned that I had biked to work. It wasn't just more rain showers like the one that caught me on the way in, but a line of tornado-spawning thunderstorms. Well, I hunkered down at the bottom of a parking deck stairwell waiting for a tornado warning to pass, and I hunkered it up at the bottle shop waiting for the rain to ease up, but in the end I didn't get drowned, electrocuted, or whisked away to Oz, just wet on #30daysofbiking Day 19: Cloudburst

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATE/TIME: Day 20
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Day 20
Apr 21, 2019, 2:47:36 PM
Day 20
It's the lazy weekend days that have ended past attempts to complete #30daysofbiking. Saturday was nearly one of those days. Drying out from day 19, tweaking some things around the house. But I knew there was good music downtown, so when the shadows started getting long, I pedaled off to @berkeley_cafe to see @sirwilliambrooks and #lestercoalbanksandthesevensorrows. A fantastic night of music, and it kept my streak going on Day 20: Night Song

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATE/TIME: Day 21
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Day 21
Apr 22, 2019, 9:47:00 AM
Day 21
Probably could have counted the post-midnight part of Saturday's ride as my Sunday #30daysofbiking requirement, but spending the day eating a peanut-butter-filled, chocolate rabbit necessitated burning some extra calories. Just wasn't sure by the time I had fattened up my tires and gotten all trolled up for the ride that I would be able to ride some Umstead paths and still be home before dark. I did make it to the far end with daylight to spare, and probably could have made it home if I hadn't detoured to go see Hellboy. So all my racing daylight was for nothing when I rode home at 10pm. Anyone notice how uprooted tree stumps can look like nefarious fairy creatures in the dark? That's probably just my movie choice. Good thing it wasn't Pet Cemetery. I don't really mind a night ride, but Hellboy was probably not worth the delay on Day 21: Here Comes the Night

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATE/TIME: Day 21_
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Day 21.
Apr 21, 2016, 3:47:53 PM
Day 21.
Time to hop on the bike and head home. Clouds that were white and fluffy earlier are starting to look a little purple. Rain is in the forecast, and it'd be nice to gett off the bike before those clouds pop. Life should give me a break considering on yesterday's ride I was almost creamed by a driver who, in a typical sign o' the times, did not even look up from her phone to see that she run a red light or that I was standing in the intersection giving her the raspberry. Beret, incidentally, is a really hard word to work into this train of thought when, if I had anything on my head, it would have been a helmet. I guess I need to let it go, pedal on home to the most beautiful girl in the world, and give her a kiss.

This is what is sounds like...

on #30daysofbiking

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Day 22
Apr 22, 2019, 4:51:45 PM
Day 22
Kind of a slog for today's commute. Don't know why. Because I left those bigger tires on there? #caseofthemondays? Is it because I didn't get enough sleep, and most of yesterday's calories came from candy and alcohol? No, probably it was the tires. No lifestyle changes required on #30daysofbiking Day 22: Come Monday

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATE/TIME: Day 25
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Day 25
Apr 26, 2019, 12:27:24 PM
Day 25
Feels like an unlucky day, but I guess if I put on my rose-coloured glasses: I made to work and home from work without getting a flat tire. I swapped bikes at home to get to my doctor's appointment, so I could use the bus partway, so my as-of-yet-unidentified, deflating, front tire did not interfere with my appointment. The spare tube I carry is sized for my much narrower wheelset, and probably wouldn't have worked in my 2.8" tire even if the tube hadn't developed several holes while kicking around in my toolkit for three years or so, so it was fortunate that there bus options, and fortunate that I figured out that I could catch the Carter-Finley rather than wait 55 minutes for the 4. So, yes, I got to the concert just in time to miss all but a minute of @jessica_stone's set, and yes, I was distracted by vain attempts to fix my tire all the way through @intothesound's set, and yes, I got home late, and tired, and grumpy, and I could chalk it up to bad luck if only I hadn't spent an hour a few weeks ago giving both of my wheelsets a once over and refreshing their sealant, except for my wide, front tire. "It's holding air, and was the most recent to be set up tubeless. Should probably leave well enough alone." Lazy negligence sounds so much meaner than bad luck. But somewhere in that mess I got in 23 miles of riding, heard some good music, and learned that @bigbossbrewing makes a tasty, amber IPA on #30daysofbiking Day 25: Fools Get Lucky

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATE/TIME: Day 26
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Day 26
Apr 27, 2019, 8:58:57 AM
Day 26
With rain chasing me on to the bus this morning and rain coming down in buckets towards the end of the work day, I thought this was going to be a low-mile day of mostly bus rides. But the rain stopped shortly after I left work, so I decided to resume the Quest For Coconut Lime Sour. Hit two bottle shops and a grocery store. Picked up some camping/bike supplies at REI. Found some friends I hadn't seen in a while, and met some new people. Found a food truck that serves chili relleno (@poblanostacos). Found another can of Everybody Wants Some at @houseofhops_raleigh, and turned a 9 mile day of riding into 20, but never found my cans of Coconut Lime on #30daysofbiking Day 26: Where Are They Now

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATE/TIME: Day 27
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Day 27
Apr 28, 2019, 11:52:32 AM
Day 27
Got my "plus-sized" tire back in operation after Day 25's failure. Easey-peasey: pry that useless tube out; insert the tubeless valve stem; try and pump the tire up fast enough with the floor pump to make it expand to the rim edge; try again; and again; and finally it works; go to add sealant through the valve stem; valve stem has a little nut that holds it in place, or it does if you remember to put it on, otherwise the valve stem just disappears into the tire, and you have to undo all you work to get it back out; much cussing; finally tire is on the rim, inflated, with sealant. Now to swap out those emaciated, 2 inch tires with the plus tires and take a little test run to make sure they're not going to let me down on the way to/from @dana_sullivan. Everything checks out, and I'm ready to head to @kingsraleigh. At some point it occurs to me that it's probably not just Dana Sullivan taking the stage. Three other bands: @brightsounds, #threebodyproblem, and #spiderbags, all of them great. It's a full night of incredible rock and roll, and I float home on some fat, rubber tires on #30daysofbiking Day 27: Big Fun

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATE/TIME: Day 29
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Day 29
Dec 20, 2025, 12:37:49 AM
Day 29
So close, and yet so far? I thought I would coast over the #30daysofbiking finish line, and maybe I will, but my bike took a turn on the way home, and I best not ride it again until it's dealt with. My little bike should be up to the challenge, but it needs some tweaks, so I've been ignoring it all month. Now I'm going to have to get those kinks worked out, since it's my main ride. Not to mention that it's now going to have carry me and my camping equipment next weekend. And a possible sacrifice will be the other goal I had this month of hitting 500 miles. I am close enough that I will make it, but only if the bike co-operates. I still think I'll make it, but this morning it seemed like a sure thing. But tonight all I know for sure is that I made it to Day 29: Bring on Tomorrow

DATE/TIME: Holiday film-fest 2019
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Holiday film-fest 2019
Dec 24, 2019, 9:50:00 PM
Holiday film-fest 2019
Thankskilling

Poultrygeist

It's a Troma film. That may be all the review this movie needs, but I'll continue. I'm including this in my holiday film list because it was part of a Thanksgiving-themed double feature, and as it involves both native Americans and poultry, it's pretty much season-appropriate, even though I'm not sure there is any direct references to the holiday. There are, however, references to every inappropriate stereotype you can imagine. The deliberate offensiveness seems to almost cancel out the wrongness of it all. By being so overt, the film seems to convey that it knows these are horrible and inappropriate jokes, and in doing so, in my mind, cuts off the obvious objections. But they still get to make the jokes. It's kind of genius. But only "kind of." I don't want to directly associate the word "genius" with this thinly-plotted collection of gross-out moments and offensive jokes. It's not a good movie, but if it's the movie you're in the mood to see, it may be a great movie. I think this may help you decide where you stand on whether this is something you want to endure or not: there's a zombie finger used as a butt plug. In other words, it's a Troma movie. If you don't know if Troma movies are your thing, this will definitely inform you.

Blood Freak

I can't even. This is B-movie madness at its best and worst. Another poultry-themed horror film, so we'll call it season-appropriate for Thanksgiving. I mean it's as appropriate there as anywhere else. I mean maybe don't watch it with grandma. On the other hand, there's a weird, religious, scared-straight vein in this film about a bloodthirsty chicken monster that might resonate with some more conservative favmily members. Watch this movie, if only because knowing that you may never want to watch it again is better then wondering if it might actually be the Thanksgiving classic you never knew you needed.

Anna and the Apocalypse

This is, hands down, the very best British, zombie-apocalypse, high school/Christmas musical that I have ever seen. And, sure, there's not a lot of competition in that space, but don't let that detract from the fact that this is a very good movie. On my second viewing, I had overcome the sheer joy at the existence of this movie, and I could see that maybe it wasn't the flawless masterpiece I had been imagining during the year between viewings. Nevertheless, it is well done, and all the better for somehow remaining a decent representation of all of those genres while still telling an engaging story. The soundtrack is similarly genre-diverse, disjointed, and thoroughly enjoyable with some straight-up Christmas songs, some straight-up moving-the-plot-along-while-we-sing-and-dance songs, some songs that function within the movie plot, but also stand on their own, and some weird, novelty songs. It's a wild mash-up of a movie, and unless you absolutely hate some of the genres involved, or simply hate All That Is Good in the World, you should probably see it.

Elf

Simply one of the best Christmas movies of all time. Really one of the best movies, period. The cast is outstanding. At the Alamo viewing, they said the title character was originally envisioned as Jim Carrey. I can see where that might be an option, but it really feels like this is the role Will Ferrell was born to play. This movie has heart and Christmas spirit and non-stop laughs. It's already a classic, and it's an annual rewatch in my house since the first time I saw it.

Christmas Blood

What you really want, or at least what I really want, in a movie about a psychopath who kills on Christmas, is a significant amount of screen time given to the killer and their exploits. And this is what is missing. It almost feels like a sequel. Several years ago a killer was captured and incarcerated over the holidays, and now he's loose and ready to kill again. But between a short scene about his original crimes and the final scenes about his return, there is a lot of set-up, character introductions, and personal drama that really feels like the bulk of the movie. For all that, the sleepy Norway town that serves as the setting is the perfect setting for this story. There's a feeling of quiet and isolation and general moodiness in the set up that plays very well, it's just a story that moves way too slowly for my taste. Still, well-filmed and well-acted and worth a little time spent watching it when you're snowed in at home and trying to fill those long, long hours waiting for your killer to finally show up.

Sugarplum

IMDB lists the budget at about $500. That seems about right. There are some performances that are passable, but not many, and some of the more elaborate costumes seemed pretty well-done. And I really enjoyed the concept. The story created around the sugar plumb fairy was pretty entertaining even if the execution didn't quite hit the mark. It's clearly not professionally done piece, but it has it's good points, just not enough of them to make a recommendation. But it's under an hour, so there's not a lot of risk in watching this.

Rare Exports

The biggest problem I have with this movie is that it came out in 2010, and I'm seeing it for the first time in 2019. That means nine Christmases have gone by when I could have, but did not, watch Rare Exports. We've seen serial killers dressed as Santa. We've seen Santa's evil counterpart, Krampus, out for blood, but this time it's Santa himself. Stories of a jolly, fat man who rewards the good children with presents and punishes the bad with coal are just watered down versions of the real history of a demon who rewards good children simply by leaving them alone. As for the bad children... Everyone is going to be sorry that foreign corporation came to dig up that ancient tomb, or is it a prison? This is a seriously dark and twisted and, occasionally, funny take on the Santa Claus myth. Fun for the whole family. Actually, no, take that R rating seriously, but do check this one out.

Klaus 12/9/19

It's a fresh, kid-friendly take on the origins of Santa Claus. Solid story and enjoyable to watch. One of the better holiday cartoons to be put out in recent memory. There are a lot of Christmas classics for children that, to my mind, have not really been matched in the last 20 or 30 years: The Rankin and Bass specials, Charlie Brown, the Grinch. It's rare that something new comes along that can hold it's own against these classics. Klaus does it, though, I think. This one may be sticking around a while.

Code Santa

I made a decision to fill the end-of-year, holiday season with seasonal films, especially those exploring the darker side of the holidays. I'm not halfway through December, but I'm tempted to end this experiment. After watching Code Santa, there is nowhere to go but down. Multiple descriptions of this film compare it, legitimately, to Home Alone. This movie came out first, so it's not a copy. It just happens to involve a boy using his wits and some handy props to defend against a home invader. While it obviously can't be derivative of Home Alone, it does owe a lot to every 80's action film starring Sylvester Stalone or Arnold Schwartzenegger. And it's all the more wonderful for all that it borrows from that genre. It is way more of an action movie than it is whatever-you-want-to-classify-Home-Alone. I would say this movie is part Die Hard, part Silent Night, Deadly Night. This is primarily a Christmas action movie, but there's some horror elements as well. Definitely a slasher film vibe, although no real gore. The violence is there, though, and this movie is dark, possibly darker than any Christmas movie I've seen because unlike so many Christmas-horror films that  fleetingly acknowledge the warmer, friendlier aspects of Christmas before heading down a different path, this movie dwells on those more traditional aspects by giving us a protagonist who still believes in Santa, and believes that his antagonist is Santa, even while he tries to protect himself and his family from the murderous home invader. If you want a more psychologically dark take on Christmas horror, wrapped up in an action movie bow, this may be your film. It's definitely mine.

Christmas Time 12/12/2019

I'm a sucker for a good time travel story. Well, I'm a sucker for a time travel story. You have to watch them to find out if they're good. And this one... well, it's watchable. The three main cast members are pretty likable and play well off of each other. Every other cast member exists only for comic relief, and unnecessarily so. There's plenty of humor without the over-the-top performances that happen every time one of the secondary performers enters the scene. Plot? Who needs it? There's a little conflict resolution. The stakes are low, as it's mostly family holiday dramedy. With time travel. Maybe. Think Happy Accidents, where we have a character who claims to time travel, but maybe they're joking or maybe they're delusional, and maybe the truth will come out by the end of the movie, but maybe it won't. And if you haven't seen Happy Accidents, by all means watch that one first as it's a much better movie. But if you want your time travel mixed up with some Christmas themes, I don't know of any others.

Slay Belles 12/14

There are some good nuggets in there, but not enough to recommend it. Barry Bostwick as Santa was kind of fun. A trio of women team up with Santa to fight Krampus. The storyline was silly, but could have been an enjoyable watch with better execution. But the story wasn't quite there. The acting wasn't quite there. Not enough good going on here.

Gremlins

What can you say? It's a classic. Cheesy, gory (if you're a gremlin), very 80s. The puppetry looks a little dated by today's standards of movie effects, but I think it holds up. It's not a movie that takes itself too seriously, so you don't have to get hung up on some dated effects, but it's also not a movie that spends all it's time making fun of itself, so I can still get immersed in the story. While it may be the most mainstream Christmas horror film, it's pretty light on the horror, and that's okay. It's a fun watch even 35 years later (good heavens I'm old!). Nostalgia, or is it really good? I'm probably not able to be objective, but I remain a fan.

A Christmas Horror Story

Some categories of bad movies: Sometimes a movie is just bad. Maybe you're sorry you watched it, or maybe not, but you won't be watching it again. Sometimes a movie is delightfully bad, and can be rewatched often. Sometimes a movie aims high and misses the mark, but contains some good ideas that it makes it worth watching. That's where I put A Christmas Horror Story. It's an anthology film, and every story seemed well-crafted enough to be enjoyable, but, unlike other anthology films I've seen, these separate, but connected storylines play out simultaneously. We're constantly switching between stories that may have some common origins, but are otherwise unrelated. It's a little disorienting, but each separate story has something worthwhile to offer. Until everything falls apart. It all unravels in a deliberate and interesting way, but in a way that also felt very unsatisfying. I expect I will be watching this again, and maybe my opinion will be improved, but for now I'm not considering this film a conceptual success, but a flop in execution. Still, a solid production in terms of filming and acting, but I didn't enjoy the editing choice to constantly move the narrative, and I didn't enjoy the resolution, or in some cases lack of resolution, of some of the otherwise interesting storylines. For the Christmas-horror genre, I think it's worth at least one watch, but that doesn't mean I think it's good.

Magician's Nephew
“I’ve got them all,” said Polly. “Look, it’s all right, I’m wearing gloves. Let’s bury them.” “Yes, let’s. I’ve marked the place where I buried the core of the Apple yesterday.” Then Polly came over the wall and they went together to the place. But, as it turned out, Digory need not have marked the place. Something was already coming up. It was not growing so that you could see it grow as the new trees had done in Narnia; but it was already well above ground. They got a trowel and buried all the magic rings, including their own ones, in a circle round it.


And the great big house in the country, which Digory had heard of all his life and never seen would now be their home; the big house with the suits of armor, the stables, the kennels, the river, the park, the hot-houses, the vineries, the woods, and the mountains behind it.


The lamp-post which the Witch had planted (without knowing it) shone day and night in the Narnian forest, so that the place where it grew came to be called Lantern Waste; and when, many years later, another child from our world got into Narnia, on a snowy night, she found the light still burning. And that adventure was, in a way, connected with the ones I have just been telling you. It was like this. The tree which sprang from the Apple that Digory planted in the back garden, lived and grew into a fine tree. Growing in the soil of our world, far out of the sound of Aslan’s voice and far from the young air of Narnia, it did not bear apples that would revive a dying woman as Digory’s Mother had been revived, though it did bear apples more beautiful than any others in England, and they were extremely good for you, though not fully magical. But inside itself, in the very sap of it, the tree (so to speak) never forgot that other tree in Narnia to which it belonged. Sometimes it would move mysteriously when there was no wind blowing: I think that when this happened there were high winds in Narnia and the English tree quivered because, at that moment, the Narnia tree was rocking and swaying in a strong southwestern gale. However that might be, it was proved later that there was still magic in its wood. For when Digory was quite middle-aged (and he was a famous learned man, a Professor, and a great traveler by that time) and the Ketterleys’ old house belonged to him, there was a great storm all over the south of England which blew the tree down. He couldn’t bear to have it simply chopped up for firewood, so he had part of the timber made into a wardrobe, which he put in his big house in the country. And though he himself did not discover the magic properties of that wardrobe, someone else did. That was the beginning of all the comings and goings between Narnia and our world, which you can read of in other books.

Surly
Aug 3, 2017, 4:48:44 PM
Surly


If you're legitimately looking at going from a Fuji to a Trucker, then I think the question becomes, "What don't you like about the Fuji, and will the Trucker address that?"




When I was in the market for a touring bike, I made list of things I wanted to change about my current bike and started looking at forums and bike reviews for my options. All things I wanted in my new bike were things the LHT had. I was made to carry a load. It had long chainstays so you didn't kick your panniers. It felt good to ride. Many people had set it up with swept back bars, which is what I wanted. It came as a bare frame, so I could put whatever I wanted on it. And I did.




The bike was great while I was riding it. There are some criticisms of the LHT, and I think they are legit, but they are generally a question of priorities. A bike made to carry everything you want is probably going to be a little sluggish. Steady and nimble do not go hand-in-hand. Those were trade-offs I was happy to make.




Surly has fun marketing. I won't deny that's part of the appeal. But the most appealing part of their message is that they seem to be making these bikes to be ridden. They don't just talk about specs and geometry and components. They talk about trips taken, loads hauled, the fun had along the way. Maybe it's not fair to other manufacturers, and/or maybe I'm giving Surly too much credit, but I've had bikes made by a variety of manufacturers, and I feel like my relationship to the company ends when I put down my money and walk away with their bike. With Surly, I see them continue to engage with the people who bought their bikes and continue to talk about what they love about their bikes and continue to, seemingly, care not just that you bought a Surly, but that you enjoy it. So I think part of the Surly "fanboi-ism" is that people buy into the marketing, but the marketing and the reality seems to be that Surly builds bikes that they, and their customers, want to ride.

*   **Not Consistent, but Clustered:** Bike problems are not a constant background noise in your logs. Instead, they appear in clusters, with the most significant being the **April 2019 flat tire incident**.
*   **Failure Types:** The primary failures are **punctures/flats** and **planning failures** (especially regarding weather and maintenance).
